coredata.dc:description: Food-borne nanoparticles (FNs) may have potential for microelement delivery due to their good biocompatibility and healthy benefits. In this paper, hydrophilic FNs with ultra-small size of 1.7 nm were prepared from the water extract solution of Auricularia auricula by hydrothermal method. The structural characterization showed that Zn(II)-FNs were formed after the interaction of abundant functional groups like amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of FNs with Zn2+. The Zn(II)-FNs showed better cell compatibility than ZnSO4 and zinc gluconate with no visible cytotoxicity at concentrations up to 75 μg/mL for the normal rat kidney cells. Less than 5% hemolysis rate was found when the concentration of Zn(II)-FNs was 5 mg/mL after incubation for 3h. The biodistribution experiments indicated that Zn(II)-FNs had no obvious toxic effect after being orally administrated at a dose of 500 mg/kg mouse body weight, and Zn(II)-FNs were present in the stomach, intestine, lung, liver, and kidney. Our data indicated that FNs collected from Auricularia auricula water extract solution might act as a safe and effective nanocarrier for Zn(II).

head.ce:title: Nanocarrier from water extract solution of Auricularia auricula for zinc delivery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food-borne nanoparticles (FNs) may have potential for microelement delivery due to their good biocompatibility and healthy benefits. In this paper, hydrophilic FNs with ultra-small size of 1.7 nm were prepared from the water extract solution of Auricularia auricula by hydrothermal method. The structural characterization showed that Zn(II)-FNs were formed after the interaction of abundant functional groups like amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of FNs with Zn2+. The Zn(II)-FNs showed better cell compatibility than ZnSO4 and zinc gluconate with no visible cytotoxicity at concentrations up to 75 μg/mL for the normal rat kidney cells. Less than 5% hemolysis rate was found when the concentration of Zn(II)-FNs was 5 mg/mL after incubation for 3h. The biodistribution experiments indicated that Zn(II)-FNs had no obvious toxic effect after being orally administrated at a dose of 500 mg/kg mouse body weight, and Zn(II)-FNs were present in the stomach, intestine, lung, liver, and kidney. Our data indicated that FNs collected from Auricularia auricula water extract solution might act as a safe and effective nanocarrier for Zn(II).

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc is one of the essential trace elements for the human body, which supports normal growth, protein metabolism, and membrane stability (Zhu et al., 2015). However, millions of people over the world may suffer from zinc deficiency mainly because of inadequate dietary intake or absorption of zinc (Praharaj et al., 2021). In general, growing children and pregnant women are at increased risk of zinc deficiency due to the high requirement for zinc (Yu et al., 2013). Different inadequate levels of zinc deficiency in humans cause several adverse effects, including gastrointestinal disorders, growth retardation, sexual development abnormalities, skin lesions, and decreased body immune function (Ali et al., 2013; Bailey et al., 2015). Therefore, daily zinc supplementation is a necessary approach to prevent zinc deficiency.

Traditional inorganic and organic zinc supplements have several disadvantages. For instance, zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) is known as the first generation of inorganic zinc supplements. However, its bioavailability could be influenced by the presence of tannins, phytate, and dietary fiber, the zinc inhibitors that impede zinc adsorption and damage the gastrointestinal tract (Dong et al., 2018; Fredlund et al., 2006). Zinc citrate and zinc gluconate, as the second generation of organic zinc supplements, only can be used for non-diabetes adults or other special groups (Wegmuller et al., 2014). In recent years, some bioactive peptides have been utilized as carriers for zinc delivery in the human body (Meng et al., 2021). However, due to the chain structure of the polypeptide, the practical application is extremely limited. So far, it has been identified that a few peptides containing serine, histidine, cysteine, aspartate, and glutamate residues can coordinate with Zn2+ (Udechukwu et al., 2016). What's more, the preparation and isolation of peptides are relatively expensive. Therefore, a safe and low-cost carrier for zinc delivery is highly needed for the development of next generation zinc supplements.

Food-borne nanoparticles (FNs) produced during thermal food processing, with strong fluorescence, have aroused a great deal of interest in recent years (Wang et al., 2020). The formation of FNs is associated with complicated physical and chemical reactions like esterification, carbonylation, and caramelization in nutritional components including proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates that occur during thermal food processing (Song et al., 2018). The formation of fluorescent FNs is similar to the top-down synthesis of carbon dots (Tan et al., 2013). The FNs have many advantages, such as ultra-small size (<10 nm), good hydrophilicity, strong fluorescence, abundant functional groups (e.g., hydroxy, amino, and carboxyl groups), higher permeability, and satisfactory biocompatibility (Wang et al., 2019). The FNs produced during thermal food processing have been used for the delivery of iron elements and doxorubicin (Duan et al., 2018; Song et al., 2021). However, the research of Zn2+ delivery with FNs originated from food-related source is hardly reported, and no detailed information of FNs from edible mushrooms as Zn(II) carriers is available in the literatures.

Auricularia auricula (A. auricula), a kind of large fungus, is rich in nutrients, including polysaccharides, proteins, polyphenols, and melanins, which has been recognized as healthy food due to its various physiological activities, including anti-oxidation, anti-cancer, and anti-inflammatory (Yu et al., 2021). There is hardly any paper reported the FNs from A. auricula water extract solution used as nanocarriers for microelement delivery. In this study, the FNs were collected from A. auricula water extract solution by the hydrothermal method and complexed with Zn2+ by one-step water bath heating method to form Zn(II)-FNs (Scheme 1 ). The morphology and optical property of Zn(II)-FNs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and fluorescence spectra. The possible interaction between FNs and Zn2+ was fully characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H-NMR). In addition, the biocompatibility of Zn(II)-FNs was evaluated through methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) and hemolysis assay. The morphological characteristics, optical properties, biocompatibility, and biodistribution of Zn(II)-FNs were also studied to explore the potential of A. auricula FNs as nanocarriers for Zn(II) delivery.

The fruiting bodies of A. auricula were purchased from Weiduobao Food Company (Suifenhe, China). D101 macroporous resin was brought from Qinshi Science and Technology Company (Zhengzhou, China). Zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4•7H2O) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich Company (Shanghai, China). The potassium bromide (KBr) was obtained from Bailingwei Company (Beijing, China). The normal rat kidney (NRK) cells were provided by Dr. Li Yu's lab at Tsinghua University, China. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was purchased from Gibco Company (New York, USA). The dimethyl-d6 sulfoxide was acquired from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Concentrated nitric acid was obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Corporation (Tianjin, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of FNs by A. auricula
ce:section.ce:para: The FNs were prepared from A. auricula extract solution by the hydrothermal method according to our previous research (Geng et al., 2019). The dried fruiting bodies of A. auricula (50 g) were washed and soaked in the distilled water for 12 h to fully absorb water. Then, a WW504 electric pressure cooker (Double Happiness, Zhuhai, China) was used to boil the fruiting bodies in distilled water (1200 mL) for 30 min. After the sample solution was cooled to 20 °C, the fruit bodies were filtered to obtain the sample solution, then the solution was concentrated to 200 mL by rotary evaporation. Then, 800 mL of ethanol was added to the solution and stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator for 12 h. The sediment was completely filtered and concentrated by rotary evaporation. When the sample was concentrated to 20 mL, the resulting solution was dropped into the column filled with D101 macroporous resin. Distilled water was used as an eluent, and the fraction with fluorescence was collected (Cao et al., 2017). The product was freeze-dried to yield 63.5 mg of FNs and stored at -20 °C for further use.

Zn(II)-FNs were synthesized by the one-step water bath heating method with some modifications (Geng et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). Briefly, FNs (100 mg) and ZnSO4 (100 mg) were dissolved in the distilled water (20 mL) and heated at 60 °C for 2 h. Then, Zn(II)-FNs were centrifuged by ultrafiltration (3000 Da) at 4 °C, 6000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the freeze-dried products were gathered to obtain 74.9 mg of Zn(II)-FNs with a yield of 34.5%.

Briefly, the freeze-dried product Zn(II)-FNs collected from 2.3 were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. The content of zinc in Zn(II)-FNs was determined by atomic absorption spectrophotometer (ZA3000, HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan). The EE and LA of Zn2+ were counted under the following equations (Yuan et al., 2021):

The size and morphological characteristics of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs were measured by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The hydrodynamic diameter of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (LS-0118-0079, LS Instruments, Switzerland). Fluorescence spectra of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs were recorded by the F-2700 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime measurement was conducted according to the previous methods (Cong et al., 2019). UV-visible spectra measurement of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs were achieved by using a Lambda 35 UV-visible spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Cambridge, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Norwalk, CT, USA) was used to analyze the changes of functional groups during the formation of Zn(II)-FNs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs were recorded with the XRD-6100 diffractometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo VG, Waltham, MA, USA) was used to investigate the elemental components of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs. The 1H NMR spectra were conducted with an AVANCE III 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker Biospin, Rheinstetten, Germany) by adding 5 mg of FNs or Zn(II)-FNs into 600 μL of dimethyl-d6 sulfoxide solution in the NMR sampling tube.

The cytotoxicity of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs in NRK cells was determined by MTT assay. At the same time, the cytotoxicity of ZnSO4 and zinc gluconate was also measured for comparison (Lee et al., 2017). The NRK cells were added into 96-well plates with 1 × 105 cells per well and cultivated in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, the NRK cells were exposed to different concentrations (25, 50, 75, 100, and 125 μg/mL) of FNs, Zn(II)-FNs, ZnSO4, and zinc gluconate for 24 h, respectively. Then, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) solutions were added into each well and cultivated for another 3 h. Subsequently, 150 μL of DMSO was added to replace the supernatant in the wells (Tie et al., 2022). After shaking for 20 min, the absorbance of solutions at 490 nm was measured with a microplate reader (Infinite 200 Multimode, Tecan, Hombrechtikon, Switzerland). The cell viability was calculated by the following formula:
(3)

where A1 was the absorbance of the NRK cells incubated with FNs, Zn(II)-FNs, ZnSO4, and zinc gluconate, respectively. A0 was the absorbance of the control group.

The male BALB/c mice (20 ± 2 g, 7-8 weeks old) were purchased from Liaoning Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd in Shenyang, China. All the protocols for the animal procedures were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Dalian Polytechnic University. The hemolysis assay was based on the literature of Jing et al. (Jing et al., 2021) with some modification. Briefly, 500 μL of whole blood was collected from healthy male BALB/c mice by eyeball extirpating. Red blood cells were washed thrice with 10 mL of PBS solution and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C to eliminate the serum proteins and debris in colorless supernatant. Then, the red blood cells were re-dissolved in 10 mL of PBS solution to prepare the red blood cells suspension. Then, 200 μL of red blood cell suspension was mixed with 800 μL of Zn(II)-FNs solution at different concentrations (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 mg/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h or 3 h, respectively. Meanwhile, distilled water was used as the positive control, and PBS solution was used as the negative control following the same method. After incubation, the intact red blood cells were separated by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min, and the absorbance of the supernatant at 570 nm was recorded by a microplate reader. The hemolysis rate was calculated by following the formula:

where Abs, Abnc, Abpc were the absorbance of Zn(II)-FNs, the negative and positive control, respectively.

After a week of acclimatization, all mice were randomly divided into 2 groups (n = 3), which were the FNs group and Zn(II)-FNs group. The mice in the FNs group were fed with FNs at a dose of 2 g/kg mouse body weight, and the mice in Zn(II)-FNs group were fed with Zn(II)-FNs at a dose of 500 mg/kg mouse body weight. Mice were sacrificed at 0.5, 2, 6, and 24 h after the FNs and Zn(II)-FNs administration and the major organs (stomach, intestine, lung, liver, and kidney) were collected. The organs were imaged on a multi-functional in vivo imaging system (Molecular Device Corporation, Sunnyvale, CA, USA) under the excitation of 365 nm wavelength (Tansi et al., 2017). Meanwhile, mice were sacrificed immediately (0 h) after being given the same dose of distilled water in the blank control group. The organs in the digestion tank were digested with 5 mL nitric acid on an electric heating plate (ECH-II, Xinyi, Shanghai, China) at 160 °C for 8 h. After digestion, the cover of the tube was opened and the solution was heated to volatilize the nitric acid until 1 mL remained. Then, the sample solution was transferred and diluted to a volume of 50 mL using deionized water. The content of zinc in different organs was determined by atomic absorption spectrophotometer and calculated by the standard curve method (Tan et al., 2010).

All the data in this study were the mean values of three parallel experiments and expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD) and analyzed using SPSS 19.0 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). All the diagrams were drafted by Origin 2018 software (OriginLab Corporation, MA, USA).

In recent years, immense amounts of research indicated that FNs with ultra-small particle size were generated during food processing (Wang et al., 2020). In this study, the FNs were prepared from A. auricula water extract solution with the hydrothermal method for the first time with a production yield of 0.127%. As shown in Fig. 1 a and c, FNs are well-dispersed spherical particles without obvious aggregation. The particle size distribution of FNs is in the range of 1-3 nm, with a mean particle size of 1.7 nm. Compared with FNs prepared from other food sources, the FNs derived from A. auricula water extract solution had a smaller size and may be more suitable as the nanocarriers for Zn2+ (Geng et al., 2019). The calculations of EE and LA were 4.9% and 15 μg/mg, respectively. Zn(II)-FNs were formed by the combination of FNs with Zn2+ and existed in the form of nanoclusters (Fig. 1b) with significantly increased particle size compared to FNs. The formation of nanoclusters was due to the mutual interaction of Zn(II) and FNs. On the one hand, it was related to the short distance between Zn(II)-FNs resulting in van der Waals forces greater than their own gravity (Xiong et al., 2021). On the other hand, it was related to the interaction of hydrogen bonds and chemical bonds on their surfaces (Whitby et al., 2018). Similar results were also observed in the complex of proteins and polysaccharides with zinc (Wang & Li, 2019; Zhang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Compared to FNs, the hydrodynamic diameter of Zn(II)-FNs measured by DLS showed a significantly increased particle size (Fig. 1d). Obviously, the slightly larger size was shown in the DLS measurements than that from TEM due to the hydrate nanoparticles in water (Yi et al., 2018). The hydrodynamic diameter was significantly increased from 67 and 148 nm for FNs to 84 and 425 nm for Zn(II)-FNs, which was consistent with the results of TEM analysis. Fan et al. also observed similar results of increased particle size in the nanoparticles after loading doxorubicin (DOX) (Fan et al., 2019).

The optical property of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs was revealed by the UV-visible spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a, FNs and Zn(II)-FNs all had two absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region. The absorption peak at 257 nm was due to the electron transitions from π to π* in CC and CN (Chen et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2020), and the absorption peak at 307 nm was probably attributed to the n–π* transitions of chromophore groups on the surface of FNs, such as CO (Ding et al., 2016). The slight red-shift and hypochromicity in the UV-visible spectra of Zn(II)-FNs revealed the interaction between FNs and Zn2+. The decrease of absorption peak intensity in UV-visible spectra may be attributed to the charge transition of Zn2+ in the ultraviolet region (Chen et al., 2013). FNs could be used as carriers of zinc due to their unique feature of the excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (Qiao et al., 2021). In line with previous studies, FNs and Zn(II)-FNs aqueous solution emitted a strong blue fluorescence (insets of Fig. 2c, d) upon the excitation of 365 nm (Mehta et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 2c, the emission maxima of FNs expressed a red-shift from 410 to 460 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 310 to 370 nm. Moreover, FNs had good pH and salt tolerance, and their fluorescence intensity was not affected by most metal ions (Fig. S1). The excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence was also found on Zn(II)-FNs (Fig. 2d). As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peaks were gradually red-shifted with a decreased intensity. The fluorescence lifetime was increased from 4.03 ns for FNs to 4.21 ns for Zn(II)-FNs (Fig. 2b), which was consistent with the study of Tammina et al. (2020)

The typical diffraction peak of amorphous FNs (2θ = 22.0°) (Fig. 3 a) indicated the highly disordered carbon atoms of the sp3 domain (Raji et al., 2019). It was found that the inorganic ZnSO4 showed a typical crystal structure spectrum with a number of sharp peaks in the measuring range. For the XRD pattern of Zn(II)-FNs, the broad XRD peak remained with some additional sharp peaks at 21.3°, 24.0°, 28.2°, and 31.7°, respectively, indicating the presence of partially ordered structure but significantly different compared to ZnSO4 (Wang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2017). To study the possible interaction between Zn2+ and FNs, the FTIR spectra of Zn(II)-FNs were recorded and compared with the pristine FNs spectra (Fig. 3b). The peak at 3383 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration mode of -OH or NH bond of FNs (Du et al., 2015; Geng et al., 2020). This band exhibited a blue-shift to 3397 cm−1 upon Zn2+ binding, which was probably attributed to the interaction of FNs with Zn2+. The absorption peak of CO bond stretching vibration at 1595 cm−1 in FNs shifted to 1600 cm−1 in Zn(II)-FNs indicated that carbonyl group was involved in the connection of FNs and Zn2+ (Lin et al., 2018). What's more, the peak at 1406 cm−1 changed to 1418 cm−1, indicating that COO- group played an essential role in binding with Zn2+ (Zhu et al., 2021). Additionally, compared to FNs spectra, the stretching vibration modes of CO and CN band in the range of 1123-1047 cm−1 were changed upon Zn2+ binding (Ba et al., 2020). The appearance of the new band at 610 cm−1, which was associated with the characteristic stretching mode of the Zn-O band, was monitored in the FTIR spectra of Zn(II)-FNs complex (Wang et al., 2020). The above results indicated the primary interaction of FNs with Zn2+ through amino nitrogen, hydroxyl, and carboxyl oxygen atoms.

The XPS measurements were performed to determine the elemental composition of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs. Fig. 3c shows that the FNs mainly contain C (281.3 eV), N (396.2 eV), O (528.2 eV) with the corresponding contents of 35.80%, 8.53%, and 54.41%, respectively (Table 1). It was consistent with the elemental composition of FNs formed during thermal processing of food in previous studies (Cong et al., 2019) and the elemental composition of FNs was related to the type of nutrients contained in A. auricula (Li et al., 2017). The high-resolution C1s spectra in Fig. S2a exhibited several characteristic peaks at 284.9, 286.4, and 288.6 eV corresponding to CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Tammina et al., 2020). The high-resolution N1s spectra in Fig. S2b exhibit two major peaks at 398.4 and 400.2 eV related to CNC and N−H, respectively (Tammina et al., 2020). The high-resolution O1s signal (Fig. S2c) manifested that the peaks at 530.1, 531.7, and 532.8 eV were for -OH, COO-, and CO (Wang et al., 2019). The elemental composition on the surface of Zn(II)-FNs was also determined from XPS analysis (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3d shows the high-resolution spectrum of Zn2p. The peaks located at 1021.9 eV and 1044.2 eV could be ascribed to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, respectively. The energy separation of Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 was about 22.3 eV, demonstrating that the existence of Zn2+ in Zn(II)-FNs and the relative content of zinc in Zn(II)-FNs was 3.5% (Table S1) (Li et al., 2019). Noteworthy, a new peak of the N-Zn bond appeared at 397.6 eV in the high-resolution spectra of N1s in Fig. S2e, which indicated the interaction between Zn2+ and amino groups. These results demonstrated that the amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups of FNs were the main binding sites for Zn2+, which were consistent with the results of the FTIR analysis. Based on the results of TEM, FTIR, and XPS analysis, we speculated the formation process of Zn(II)-FNs nanoclusters in Fig. 3e. The formation of Zn(II)-FNs was the result of the connection between FNs and Zn2+ through N on amino group, O on hydroxyl and carboxyl group, then Zn(II)-FNs were attracted to each other by van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, and chemical bonds together to induce agglomeration and form nanoclusters.

1H NMR spectra were also studied to further reveal the interaction between zinc and FNs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the resonance peak at 2.5 ppm was DMSO-d6 (Huang & Hsieh, 2010). The bimodal signal of methylene at 1.101 and 1.117 ppm in Fig. 4b were shifted to 1.221 and 1.239 ppm after the formation of Zn(II)-FNs (Song et al., 2019). The signal peak of protons in R3CH at 1.844 ppm in Fig. 4c moved 0.120 ppm to the direction of the strong magnetic field, which was due to the connection between FNs and Zn2+. As shown in Fig. 4d, the signal of alcohols and ethers bonds in FNs appeared at 4.872 and 4.881 ppm, then shifted to 4.876 and 4.886 ppm in Zn(II)-FNs. The bimodal signal at 5.435 and 5.454 ppm (Fig. 4e) attributed to 1H in the hydroxyl group of FNs was shifted to 5.439 and 5.458 ppm. Besides, the bimodal signal at 7.411 and 7.430 ppm (Fig. 4f) corresponding to aromatic ring protons were shifted to 7.385 and 7.404 ppm after the Zn2+ interaction. The N-H signal of the amide bond (7.982 ppm) and the signal peak of the proton in the nitrogen-containing heterocycle (8.088 and 8.471 ppm) were disappeared in Zn(II)-FNs (Jayanthi et al., 2019), which was shown in Fig. 4g-i. The obvious chemical shifts indicated that Zn2+ were coordinated with FNs through amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups.

The cell compatibility of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs was determined by the MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the FNs derived from water extract solution of A. auricula after hydrothermal treatment had no obvious cytotoxicity within the experimental concentration range (25, 50, 75, 100, 125 μg/mL) to NRK cells. The non-toxicity of FNs was the key factor to be used as Zn2+ carriers (Ciappellano et al., 2016). As the concentration increased from 25 to 125 μg/mL, the cell viability (Fig. 5b) gradually decreased after the treatment of Zn(II)-FNs, ZnSO4, and zinc gluconate. The cytotoxicity was decreased in the order of ZnSO4, zinc gluconate, and Zn(II)-FNs. It had been reported that the cytotoxicity of zinc salt depended on its type, and ZnSO4 showed higher cytotoxicity than zinc gluconate (Pavlica et al., 2009). When NRK cells were treated with 75 μg/mL Zn(II)-FNs and zinc gluconate, the cell viability remained above 90%, but the cell viability decreased to 31% after treatment with ZnSO4. Furthermore, the cell viability was as high as 72% after 100 μg/mL Zn(II)-FNs treatment, while the cell viability was drastically reduced to 45% after zinc gluconate treatment at the same concentration. The results indicated that the Zn(II)-FNs had higher cell compatibility, compared with ZnSO4 and zinc gluconate.

Hemolysis is the lysis of the cytoplasmic membrane of red blood cells, and the release of hemoglobin from red blood cells into the surrounding medium (Majumdar et al., 2021). Hemolysis occurs when the toxic foreign matter comes into contact with blood (Seyfert et al., 2002). For the effective application of Zn(II)-FNs, it is necessary to evaluate the blood compatibility of Zn(II)-FNs by the test with red blood cells. Less than 5% is generally considered to be a relatively safe value for the hemolysis rate (Song et al., 2017). Fig. 5c shows the picture of red blood cells centrifuged in the micro-centrifuge tube after incubating with different concentrations (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 mg/mL) of Zn(II)-FNs for 1 h and 3 h, respectively. Hemolysis was observed when red blood cells were exposed to distilled water (positive control). Compared with the positive control, there was no significant hemolysis when the red blood cells were exposed to Zn(II)-FNs. As shown in Fig. 5d, the hemolysis rate of red blood cells was concentration-dependent and increased with the addition of concentration of Zn(II)-FNs. Fortunately, the hemolysis rate of red blood cells treated with various concentration Zn(II)-FNs for 1 h or 3 h was less than 5%. When the cells were incubated with 5 mg/mL nanoparticles for 3 hours, the hemolysis rate was only 0.68%. The results were consistent with that of doxorubicin-loaded keratin nanoparticles by Li et al. (Li et al., 2017) and indicated that Zn(II)-FNs did not disturb red blood cells membranes and hemolysis in the blood system, which had excellent blood compatibility.

Herein, in vivo biodistribution experiment was performed to explore the distribution of Zn(II)-FNs in major organs of mice at different time after oral administration (Wang et al., 2019). The fluorescence signal distribution of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs in the major organs of mice was recorded by calculating the average signal of interest of area (Fig. 6 a). No significant acute toxicity in mice was found after oral administration of FNs and Zn(II)-FNs at experimental doses (500 mg/kg mouse body weight). The fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6b) and zinc content (Fig. 6 c) were detected in organs, including the stomach, intestine, lung, liver, and kidney. It was found that Zn(II)-FNs showed the strongest fluorescence signal and elemental zinc content in the stomach at 0.5 h and in the intestine at 2 h after oral administration, indicating that Zn(II)-FNs were rapidly transferred to the stomach, and then in the intestine after 2 h. With the increase of the digestion time to 24 h, the fluorescence signal intensity of other organs except for the lung gradually decreased, indicating that Zn(II)-FNs could be consumed or eliminated from the body through the metabolism in mice. The distribution of Zn(II)-FNs in the organs of mice was related to the digestion of the gastrointestinal tract and the transport of body fluids, including blood and lymph (Song et al., 2019).

In summary, the FNs were prepared from A. auricula water extract solution by the hydrothermal method, which could be used as a safe nanocarrier of zinc. Functional groups on the surface of FNs, such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, may play a crucial role in combination with Zn2+. As a zinc supplement, Zn(II)-FNs had good cell and blood compatibility. The Zn(II)-FNs had no obvious acute toxicity after oral administration and were distributed in the stomach, intestine, lung, liver, and kidney of mice. This finding suggested that the FNs from A. auricula water extract solution by the hydrothermal method might be used as a potential nanocarrier for microelement zinc delivery.
